assignment_description:
Color Theory: Introducing Phthalo Blue, Quinacridone Gold & Quinacridone Red
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palettes prepared with new primary colors Phthalo Blue, Quinacridone Gold & Quinacridone Red, brushes, water, cloth, printed color wheel

PREP: Print on cardstock
∞ Link: Color Wheel

➜ RECAP
• Recall all you know about color thus far.
• Name the primary colors that you used in previous levels.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Different Primaries = Different Rainbows L7 L1

➜ INTRO
We will use new primary colors in Levels 7 & 8: Phthalo Blue, Quinacridone Gold & Quinacridone Red. New primary colors will create a completely new rainbow. Let’s create a new color wheel as we begin to explore our new colors. We have two lessons to complete this.

➜ PRACTICE
• New color wheel (Complete over two lesson periods; work at your own pace.)
• Paint primary colors first; then secondary; then tertiary.
• Students will need to mix fresh secondary and tertiary colors.
• Refer to the example to see placement within the wheel.
• Color placement on the color wheel must be exact. Start over if you make a mistake.
• The color wheel will be referred to all term so keep it in a handy place. One idea is to paste it onto the inside cover of your Art Book.
∞ Image Link: Example of Expanded Color Wheel

Color Theory: New Expanded Color Wheel
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palettes, brushes, water, cloth, color wheel

➜ RECAP
• What are our new primary colors? (Phthalo Blue, Quinacridone Gold & Quinacridone Red)
• What were the primary colors in previous levels? (Quinacridone Pink, Prussian Blue, and Hansa Yellow)

➜ PRACTICE
• Complete the new color wheel.
• Finish mixing colors and painting them in the appropriate places.
• Refer to the example if you are confused about placement within the wheel.
• Color placement on the color wheel must be exact. Start over if you make a mistake.
• The color wheel will be referred to all term so keep it in a handy place. One idea is to paste it onto the inside cover of your Art Book.
∞ Image Link: Example of Expanded Color Wheel

• OCCUPATIONS Practice color matching in your nature journal.

Color Theory: Playing with Different Color Wheels
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palettes, brushes, water, cloth, printed color wheel

PREP: Print on cardstock
∞ Link: Color Wheel

➜ RECAP
• Recall all you know about color thus far.
• Name the primary colors that you used in previous levels.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Different Primaries = Different Rainbows L7 L1

➜ INTRO
We will use new primary colors in Levels 7 & 8: Phthalo Blue, Quinacridone Gold & Quinacridone Red. New primary colors will create a completely new rainbow. Let’s create a new color wheel as we begin to explore our new colors.

➜ PRACTICE
• Make other color wheels by mixing your previous primaries with the new primaries. Anytime you sub one primary for another, you will get a completely new rainbow. So if you kept Phthalo Blue and Quinacridone Gold, but used Quinacridone Pink, you would get different oranges and purples. If you used Prussian Blue and mixed it with Quinacridone Red, you would get a different purple, etc. Just be sure to keep your experimental palette separate from your working palette.
• Label your new Rainbow with the primaries that you used.

Color Theory: Playing with Different Color Wheels
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palettes, brushes, water, cloth, printed color wheel

PREP: Print on cardstock
∞ Link: Color Wheel

➜ RECAP
• Recall all you know about color thus far.
• Name the primary colors that you used in previous levels.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Different Primaries = Different Rainbows L7 L1

➜ INTRO
We will use new primary colors in Levels 7 & 8: Phthalo Blue, Quinacridone Gold & Quinacridone Red. New primary colors will create a completely new rainbow. Let’s create a new color wheel as we begin to explore our new colors.

➜ PRACTICE
• Make another new color wheel by mixing your previous primaries with the new primaries. Anytime you sub one primary for another, you will get a completely new rainbow. So if you kept Phthalo Blue and Quinacridone Gold, but used Quinacridone Pink, you would get different oranges and purples. If you used Prussian Blue and mixed it with Quinacridone Red, you would get a different purple, etc. Just be sure to keep your experimental palette separate from your working palette.
• Label your new Rainbow with the primaries that you used.
• You should have at least four different purples, greens, and oranges at this point from your new color wheels. All of your tertiary colors will be different as well. You have all of these different colors just by using two different blues, yellows, and reds. Adding in neutrals expands your colors even more!

Color Theory: Mixing Browns
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall tertiary colors.
• Recall complementary colors.

➜ INTRO
Students will make browns from their new color wheels.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Mixing Browns L7 L5

➜ PRACTICE
• Mix two different browns using yellow & purple and yellow-green & red-purple. Paint the new brown in the appropriate swatch.
• Label your new browns so that you remember how to mix them when you need them.

➜ DISCUSS
What did you observe about your colors?

Color Theory: Mixing Browns
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall tertiary colors.
• Recall complementary colors.

➜ INTRO
Students will continue to make browns from their new color wheel.

➜ PRACTICE
• Mix two browns using green & red and blue-green & red-orange. Paint the brown in its proper swatch.
• Label your new browns so that you remember how to mix them when you need them.

➜ DISCUSS
What do you observe about your colors?

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for and collect items on your nature walk that are variations of one color.

Color Theory: Mixing Brown
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book, a flower with leaves and a stem

➜ RECAP
Recall tertiary colors.

➜ PRACTICE
• Mix browns using blue & orange and blue-purple & yellow-orange today. Paint them in their appropriate swatch.
• Label your new browns so that you remember how to mix them when you need them.

➜ DISCUSS
What did you observe about your colors?

• OCCUPATIONS Do this same activity in your Art Book with pastels.

Color Theory: Value Scales
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book, pencil

➜ RECAP
Recall warm and cool colors.

➜ INTRO
Students will create warm and cool value scales. This lesson will take two sessions to complete.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Warm and Cool Value Scales L7 L8

➜ PRACTICE
Create value scales for secondary colors (orange, green, and purple).
• Use a pencil to lightly sketch three value scales in your Art Book. Each value scale should have eleven 1” squares.
• Start with each secondary color in two clean wells.
• Paint the middle square in the first scale orange.
• Dip the very tip of our brush into Quinacridone Red. (Use only a tiny amount so you get a gradual change.) Mix into one of your orange wells. Paint the next square (on the right side). Repeat this step five times.
• Do this same process on the other side of your orange value scale, but add a tiny amount of Quinacridone Gold and use the second well of fresh orange.
• Create a value scale for green. Repeat steps above. Add Phthalo Blue on one side and then Quinacridone Gold on the other side.
• Create a value scale for purple. Repeat steps above. Add Quinacridone Red to one side and Phthalo Blue to the other side.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell the process you went through to change the value of secondary colors.

Color Theory: Value Scales
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
Recall warm and cool colors.

➜ INTRO
Students will continue creating warm and cool value scales.

➜ PRACTICE
Finish creating value scales for secondary colors (orange, green, and purple).

➜ DISCUSS
Tell the process you went through to change the value of secondary colors.

Color Theory: Color Intensity
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book, pencil

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall how to change the value by adding Neutral.

➜ INTRO
Today, we are going to learn about color intensity. Color intensity refers to the brightness or dullness of a color.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Color Intensity Scales with Complementaries L7 L10

➜ PRACTICE
• Lightly sketch 12 color scales with five 1” squares in each.
• Begin by painting the pure hue (color) into the first square on the scale.
• Slowly add a tiny amount of its complement into the color and paint the next square. Do this a total of four times.
• Paint scales for Quinacridone Red, red-orange, orange, and yellow-orange today.

➜ DISCUSS
Walk through the process you went through and share your thoughts with your teacher.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for and collect items on your nature walk that are variations of one color.

Color Theory: Intensity Scales
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall how to change the value by adding Neutral.
• Recall color intensity (the brightness or dullness of a color).

➜ INTRO
Today, we will paint color intensity scales for Quinacridone Gold, yellow-green, green, and blue-green.

➜ PRACTICE
• Begin by painting the pure hue (color) into the first square on the scale.
• Slowly add a tiny amount of its complement into the color and paint the next square. Do this a total of four times.
• Paint scales for Quinacridone Gold, yellow-green, green, and blue-green today.

➜ DISCUSS
Walk through the process and share your thoughts with your teacher.

Color Theory: Color Intensity Scales
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, Titanium White, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ NOTE
Students will need white in the palette. If you haven’t already, please add white to a clean well and let it dry for 24-48 hours before the next lesson.

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall how to change the value by adding Neutral.
• Recall color intensity (the brightness or dullness of a color).

➜ INTRO
Today, we will paint color intensity scales for Phthalo Blue, blue-purple, purple, and red-purple.

➜ PRACTICE
• Begin by painting the pure hue (color) into the first square on the scale.
• Slowly add a tiny amount of its complement into the color and paint the next square. Do this a total of four times.
• Create scales with Phthalo Blue, blue-purple, purple, and red-purple today.

➜ DISCUSS
Walk through the process you went through and share your thoughts with your teacher.

Color Theory: Color Tone Scales
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book, pencil

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall how to change the value by adding Neutral.
• Recall intensity.

➜ INTRO
Today, we are going to learn about color tone. Color tone is mixing a hue (color) with gray to lessen its colorfulness.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Toning Down Colors with Gray L7 L13

➜ PRACTICE
• Lightly sketch 12 color scales with five 1” squares in each.
• Students will need to mix a gray in their palettes. You will use a lot of gray, so mix a substantial amount.
• Begin by painting the pure hue (color) into the first square on the scale.
• Slowly add a tiny amount of gray into the color and paint the next square. Do this a total of four times.
• Create scales with Quinacridone Red, red-orange, and orange today.

➜ DISCUSS
Walk through the process and share your thoughts with your teacher.

Color Theory: Color Tone Scales
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP
• Recall value and shading.
• Recall how to change the value by adding Neutral.
• Recall intensity.

➜ INTRO
Today, we are going to continue working on color tone.

➜ PRACTICE
Create scales with yellow-orange and Quinacridone Gold today.

➜ DISCUSS
Walk through the process and share your thoughts with your teacher.

Color Theory: Color Tone Scales
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP

➜ RECAP

Color Theory: Color Tone
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ RECAP

Color Theory: The Watercolor Value Sketch
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ NOTE
You will work through this lesson again, utilizing your new primary colors.
In this tutorial, she uses several different blacks or grays out of the tube. You will only use neutral. However, you should try to mix your own blacks from the colors you have available in your palette.

➜ RECAP
Recall neutral and creating shades of colors.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: The Watercolor Value Sketch
• Watch the tutorial in full. This will take two lesson times.

Color Theory: The Watercolor Value Sketch
⍞ Materials: spray bottle, watercolor palette, brushes, water, cloth, Art Book

➜ NOTE
In this tutorial, she uses several different blacks or grays out of the tube. You will only use neutral. However, you should try to mix your own blacks from the colors you have available in your palette, adding in new primary colors.

➜ RECAP
Recall neutral and creating shades of colors.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: The Watercolor Value Sketch
• Watch the tutorial in full. This will take two lesson times.

Color Theory: The Watercolor Value Sketch
⍞ Materials: brush, watercolors, water, cloth, art book, simple landscape photo

➜ NOTE
You will work on this for two lessons.

➜ RECAP
Recall neutral and creating shades of colors.

➜ PRACTICE
• Recall the beginning of the tutorial when she flipped through her different versions of the same painting.
∞ Video Link: The Watercolor Value Sketch
• She referred to her paintings as notan paintings.
• Notan (pronounced no-tan)= a Japanese term that means 'light and dark harmony/balance.' It is a black & white version of a work of art.
• You need a photo of a simple landscape. Try to find one similar to the one she used, making sure there are no people or animals and only one distant, simple structure, if any.
• You will spend this lesson time and the next creating several notan paintings of the same simple landscape.

Color Theory: The Watercolor Value Sketch
⍞ Materials: brush, watercolors, water, cloth, art book, simple landscape photo

➜ NOTE
You will work on this for two lessons.

➜ RECAP
• Recall neutral and creating shades of colors.
• Recall Notan.

➜ PRACTICE
• Create several more simple notan paintings of your landscape photo. Refer to the video as needed.
∞ Video Link: The Watercolor Value Sketch
• Try to time yourself and complete one every five minutes. Remember, you are looking for the values and masses- no details.
• Look through all of the versions you have created. How did you do? Which one is your favorite? Why?

➜ DISCUSS
What techniques did you use to apply value?

Color Theory: Portrait in Notan
⍞ Materials: brush, watercolors, water, cloth, art book, black & white photo of a person (bust- shoulders, neck, and head)

➜ RECAP
• Recall neutral and creating values
• Recall Notan.
• Recall how to mix colors to achieve a black.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Easier Watercolor Portraits

➜ PRACTICE
• Watch the tutorial video.
• Paint your portrait using one of the black/gray families you have made from your palette.

➜ DISCUSS
What techniques did you use to apply value?

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.

Questions will come from:
Art Instruction: Level 7
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Art: Level 7 — TERM 1 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Explain how changing your primary colors affects your palette.

· Explain what tonal value is and how to appropriately use your erasers in a tonal drawing.

· What happens to a hue (aka Color) when you add gray to it?

Brush Drawing: Brush Stroke Review
⍞ Materials: Brush Drawing as Applied to Natural Forms by May Mallam, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, flowers

PREP: Read the lesson and preview the plate.
“Plate II” Brush Drawing

➜ NOTE
You will need to gather trumpet and cone-shaped flowers, i.e., pansies, morning glories, lilies, beardtongue, bignonias, etc., for the next brush drawing lesson.

➜ RECAP
Review brush care and use.

➜ INTRO
Today, we are going to review brush strokes. It is important to do this review at the beginning of the term as a refresher.

➜ PRACTICE
• Practice brush strokes from “Plate II” until the student is able to do the stroke with ease.
• Look at objects around your classroom and brush draw those objects; i.e., chairs, books, candlesticks, etc.
• Change colors as the student desires.
“Plate II” Brush Drawing

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Which brush strokes look best?

➜ NOTE
Students should clean up at the end of each lesson. Clean, dry, and store the brushes. Leave the palette open to dry.

• OCCUPATIONS Practice brush drawing different leaves from memory.
Continue to strengthen your brush stroke by beautifully writing out the alphabet.

Brush Drawing: Cone-Shaped Flowers
⍞ Materials: Brush Drawing as Applied to Natural Forms by May Mallam, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, flowers

PREP: Read the lesson and preview the plate
“Plate XVI” Brush Drawing

➜ NOTE
You will need to gather trumpet and cone-shaped flowers, i.e., pansies, morning glories, lilies, beardtongue, bignonias, etc. It is preferable that your student work from life, but if it is not possible, please refer to the linked images for examples. There is a video if you need to see an example of how to brush draw a flower from life.
∞ Image Link: Cone Shaped Flowers #1
∞ Image Link: Cone Shaped Flowers #2
∞ Image Link: Cone Shaped Flowers #3

➜ RECAP
• Review brush care and use.
• Recall mass drawing from Plate III.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS (Optional)
∞ Video Link: Observational Brush Drawing of a Flower L1 L39, L2 L40, & L7 L26

➜ PRACTICE
• Work through the lesson on “Plate XVI.”
• Mix all of your colors and paint color swatches before beginning.
• Do observational brush drawings of cone-shaped flowers.
“Plate XVI” Brush Drawing

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Look for and collect flowers on your nature walk.

• OCCUPATIONS Begin creating a design on a nice quality piece of paper. Use trumpet flowers for the border or brush draw a mass of flowers in the center. After it’s had a few days to dry, you can write a poem or favorite quote and give it as a gift to someone. Plan to work on this all term.

Brush Drawing: Conical Flowers
⍞ Materials: Watercolors, water, brushes, cloth, Art Book, flowers

➜ RECAP
• Review brush care and use.
• Recall the flowers you worked with last week.

➜ NOTE
Remember that in repeating lessons, you are strengthening muscles and growing in your mastery of brush drawing.

➜ PRACTICE
• Choose a new flower from the same types of flowers as last lesson.
• Observe your flower closely.
• Discuss observations.
• Mix all of your colors and paint color swatches before beginning.
• Brush draw your flower, leaves, stems, and buds. Be careful to paint what you see and not what you think you see.

➜ DISCUSS
Share your drawings with your teacher.

• OCCUPATIONS Continue to add to your design on a nice quality piece of paper. Use trumpet flowers for the border, or brush draw a mass of flowers in the center. After it’s had a few days to dry, you can write a poem or a favorite quote and give it as a gift to someone.

⍞ Materials: Watercolors, water, brushes, cloth, Art Book, flowers

PREP: You will need flowers with distinct petals today, i.e., vinca, clematis, or daisy, etc. The specimen should include the flower, a stem with leaves, and preferably a bud.
∞ Links: Red Vinca, Clematis, Daisy

➜ RECAP
Review brush care and use.

➜ PRACTICE

• Discuss observations.
• Mix all of your colors and paint color swatches before beginning.
• Brush draw your flower, leaves, stems, and buds. Be careful to paint what you see and not what you think you see.
• Share your drawings with your teacher.

Brush Drawing: Paint a Scene from History or Literature
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, Art Book

➜ RECAP
Recall underpainting.

➜ PRACTICE
Paint a scene from a recent history or literature lesson. Begin with an underpainting. Apply all of the drawing and watercolor skills that you have learned over the years.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell about your painting.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Talk about the brush strokes that you see in picture study.

Brush Drawing: Lettering
⍞ Materials: Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes

PREP: Read “Lettering” & “Development” p.161-164.

➜ RECAP

➜ STUDY, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Lettering “Plate 83” p.165

➜ NOTE
There are many types of lettering in “Plate 83.” Students are to practice the whole alphabet (or number series) for each type. Students have three lessons to complete this.

➜ PRACTICE
Lettering

• OCCUPATIONS Create a design on cardstock and use your brush to write a beautiful quote or favorite poem. This will take you several weeks to complete.

Brush Drawing: Monograms
⍞ Materials: Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes

PREP: Read “Spacing” p.167-170.

➜ WARM UP
Make lines in all directions on scrap paper.

➜ STUDY, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Monograms and Initial Letters
“Plate 85” p.169

➜ PRACTICE
• Practice creating monograms and initial letters in your Art Book.
• Use the first letter of your name to create a beautiful brush drawing.

• OCCUPATIONS Fold a piece of white cardstock in half. Use the first letter of a friend’s name to create a beautiful brush drawing on the front flap and write a letter to them inside.

Brush Drawing: Illuminated Manuscript
⍞ Materials: Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, cardstock, pencil, ruler, waterproof ink pen, metallic waterproof ink pens (optional), scrap paper

➜ NOTE
This will last through two Brush Drawing lessons

➜ WARM UP
Make strokes in all directions on a scrap piece of paper.

➜ PRACTICE
• Based on the monograms practiced in Lesson 37, you will design an
illuminated manuscript.
• Look at examples.
∞ Image Link: Illuminated Manuscript Example 1
∞ Image Link: Illuminated Manuscript Example 2
∞ Image Link: Illuminated Manuscript Example 3
• Create a border around your paper by drawing a box in pencil one inch from the outside edge of your paper all the way around.
• Create your letter as large as possible inside this box. It can punch through the frame ever so slightly. For example, if your letter is a capital R, the foot may bust through the frame to the right.
• Draw a design in the frame outside of the letter. Then, create a different design in the background of the letter or the letter itself, but not both. Leave one of them solid.
• Remember design lines or look back at Plates 67 & 68 on p.128-129.

Brush Drawing: Illuminated Manuscript
Materials: Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work, brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, cardstock, pencil, ruler, waterproof ink pen, metallic waterproof ink pen (optional)

➜ NOTE
This will last through two Brush Drawing lessons

➜ WARM UP
Make strokes in all directions on a scrap piece of paper.

➜ PRACTICE
• Based on the monograms practiced in Lesson 37, you will design an illuminated manuscript.
• Look at examples.
∞ Image Link: Illuminated Manuscript Example 1
∞ Image Link: Illuminated Manuscript Example 2
∞ Image Link: Illuminated Manuscript Example 3
• Create a border around your paper by drawing a box in pencil one inch from the outside edge of your paper all the way around.
• Create your letter as large as possible inside this box. It can punch through the frame ever so slightly. For example, if your letter is a capital R, the foot may bust through the frame to the right.
• Draw a design in the frame outside of the letter. Then create a different design in the background of the letter or the letter itself, but not both. Leave one of them solid.
• Remember design lines or look back at Plates 67 & 68 on p.128-129.
• Fill your design in with watercolors. Outline all pencil lines that you want to be part of your design with waterproof ink after the watercolor has dried.
• Fill in some areas with your metallic ink if you have it.

• OCCUPATIONS Create a beautiful alphabet as a gift for someone you love.

Brush Drawing: Watercolor Techniques
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, masking fluid, white crayons, masking tape, cotton balls, bubble wrap, paper towel

➜ RECAP
Recall graduated washes and lifting.

➜ NOTE
You will work while watching the tutorial video on watercolor techniques for this lesson. You will need to pause and start a few times.

➜ PRACTICE
Watercolor techniques.
∞ Video Link: Lifting & Resist (Masking) L6 L17 & L7 L37

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Think about ways to incorporate your knowledge of watercolor techniques in your nature journaling.

• OCCUPATIONS Use the new techniques that you have learned in making an advertising poster for a handy product.

Brush Drawing: Wet-on-Wet Watercolor Technique
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, watercolor paper or cardstock

➜ RECAP
Recall layering and the wet-on-wet technique.

➜ PRACTICE
Use the wet-on-wet watercolor technique to paint a sunset from memory.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Pay attention to the sunset and discuss your observations.

• OCCUPATIONS Do a sunset painting on nice paper using the wet-on-wet technique.

Brush Drawing: Dry-on-Wet Technique
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, watercolor paper or cardstock

➜ RECAP
• Recall mass drawing with the brush.
• Recall dry-on-wet technique.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Watercolor Technique Dry into Wet (YouTube)

➜ PRACTICE
Create a landscape, either working from memory or observation. Remember to use the dry-on-wet technique.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Think about ways to incorporate your knowledge of watercolor techniques in your nature journaling.

Brush Drawing: Still Life
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, objects with texture for a still life, light source

➜ RECAP
• Recall mass drawing with the brush.
• Recall dry brush watercolor technique.

➜ PRACTICE
• Set up an interesting still life. Fix your light source so that it is not shining directly on your objects.
• Discuss observations. How could you apply the dry brush technique to add texture?
• Use layering, gradual washes, adding Neutral, and other techniques in order to apply value to your brush drawing. Don’t forget the shadows.

• OCCUPATIONS Do another landscape painting using this technique on nice paper.

Brush Drawing: Masking
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, watercolor paper or cardstock, waterproof ink pen, metallic waterproof ink pen (optional)

➜ RECAP
Recall masking.

➜ NOTE
This project will last two Brush Drawing lessons.

➜ PRACTICE
Begin using the watercolor techniques you have learned to create an advertising poster for a handy product.
• Look at the examples.
∞ Image Links: Advertisement Examples 1-3
• Decide on a product that you want to advertise.
• Lay out your design lightly in pencil.
• Trace over the main lines with waterproof ink.
• Fill in color with your watercolors.
• Remember to use masking where you want your design to remain as white as the paper.

Brush Drawing: Masking
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, watercolor paper or cardstock, waterproof ink pen, metallic waterproof ink pen (optional)

➜ RECAP
Recall masking.

➜ NOTE
This project will last two Brush Drawing lessons.

➜ PRACTICE
Finish your advertising poster.
• Look at the examples.
∞ Image Links: Advertisement Examples 1-3
• Decide on a product that you want to advertise.
• Lay out your design lightly in pencil.
• Trace over the main lines with waterproof ink.
• Fill in color with your watercolors.
• Remember to use masking where you want your design to remain as white as the paper.

Brush Drawing: Watercolor & Salt
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, watercolor paper or cardstock, salt (table, sea, coarse, etc.)

➜ RECAP
Recall all of the watercolor techniques that you can remember.

➜ NOTE
This project will last two Brush Drawing lessons.

➜ PRACTICE
• Watch the video tutorial.
∞ Video Links: Watercolor & Salts
• Work through the charting as displayed in the tutorial. Create a column on your paper for each type of salt you have. If you only have one type of salt, you will only need one column.
• Next lesson, you will apply the salt method to your painting examples.

Brush Drawing: Watercolor & Salt
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, watercolor paper or cardstock, salt (table, sea, coarse, etc.)

➜ RECAP
Recall all of the watercolor techniques that you can remember.

➜ NOTE
This project will last two Brush Drawing lessons.

➜ PRACTICE
• Watch the video tutorial.
∞ Video Links: Watercolor & Salts
• Work through the two sample projects like in the tutorial.

Brush Drawing: Watercolor Free Draw
⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, watercolor paper or cardstock, and any extra media you would like to add in

➜ RECAP
Recall all of the watercolor techniques that you can remember.

➜ NOTE
You have learned many new ideas in this and the last term. Take the next two lesson times to create your own works of art, applying the knowledge you have gained.

➜ PRACTICE
Create works of art based on your own interest, applying the knowledge you have gained.

⍞ Materials: Brushes, water, cloth, spray bottle, watercolor palettes, watercolor paper or cardstock, and any extra media you would like to add in

assignment_description:
Drawing: Draped Fabric
⍞ Materials: Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work, charcoal, Art Book, drawing board, smooth fabric, lamp or spotlight, pink pearl and kneaded erasers

PREP: Gather 1 yard of solid color, smooth fabric.

➜ RECAP
• Recall how to set up your drawing board.
• Recall shading, light source, shadows, and highlights.
• Recall how to create values with charcoal.
• Recall how to use your pink pearl and kneaded erasers as drawing tools.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Drawing Draped Fabric L7 L49

➜ PRACTICE
• Look at “Plate 59” to see an image of draped fabric.
• Drape your fabric over the back of a chair. Don’t be particular about the fold, just let it lie how it falls.
• Set up your light source so that it is shining across your fabric.
• Draw your fabric in your Art Book using charcoal. Look at the rolling shades. Do you notice that where the folds are, the value goes from very dark to light?
• Use your erasers as drawing tools, not to erase mistakes.
• You may include the chair if you would like, but your focus must be on drawing fabric.
• Share your drawing with your teacher.
“Plate 59” p.102

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Observe fabric all around you (blankets, curtains, clothing, etc.)

• OCCUPATIONS Do brush drawings of draped fabric.

Drawing: Composition
⍞ Materials: Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work, charcoal, Art Book, drawing board, smooth fabric, lamp or spotlight, other simple objects for still lifes, pink pearl and kneaded erasers

PREP: Read “Pictorial Composition” p.117-120.

➜ RECAP
• Recall how to set up your drawing board.
• Recall shading, light source, shadows, and highlights.
• Recall how to create values with charcoal.
• Recall thumbnail sketches.
• Recall how to use your pink pearl and kneaded erasers as drawing tools.

➜ PRACTICE
• Study “Plate 65.”
• Set up an interesting still life by draping your fabric over the table and placing objects on top of it. Make sure that you have spots where the fabric is gathered and folded.
• Place your light source so that it is shining on your objects.
• Set up four thumbnails on your paper.
• Watch tutorial on composition.
• Try out four different compositions in your thumbnails before settling on one.
• Pick your most interesting composition and draw it on a clean sheet of paper with charcoal and erasers.
• Share your drawing with your teacher.
“Plate 65” p.118
∞ Video Link: Composition L7 L50

• OCCUPATIONS Read through this Art Renewal Center blog post to learn more about composition and mass drawing.
∞ Article Link: How to Create a Classic Still Life in Pastel

Drawing: Composition
⍞ Materials: Pastels, Art Book, drawing board, smooth fabric, lamp or spotlight, other simple objects for still lifes, pink pearl and kneaded erasers

➜ RECAP
• Recall shading, light source, shadows, and highlights.
• Recall thumbnail sketches.
• Recall how to use your pink pearl and kneaded erasers as drawing tools.

➜ PRACTICE
• You will use pastels to introduce color to your drawing.
• Set up a different, interesting still life by draping your fabric over a chair and placing your objects in front of it.
• Place your light source so that it is shining on your objects.
• Set up four thumbnails on your paper.
• Try out four different compositions in your thumbnails before settling on one.
• Pick your most interesting composition and draw it on a clean sheet of paper with pastels and erasers.
• Continue to do mass drawings, even while using color.
• Remember that you are using color but adding value and shading. You are not coloring your picture as though it were a coloring book.

➜ DISCUSS
Share your drawing with your teacher.

• OCCUPATIONS Do brush drawings of draped still life and fabric. Remember to try out several compositions before settling on one.

Drawing: Objects with Texture
⍞ Materials: Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work, charcoal, Art Book, erasers, 8 objects with 8 different textures

PREP: Read “Textures” Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work p.176-179.

➜ RECAP
Recall line and pattern.

➜ PRACTICE
• Gather together 8 different objects with different textures.
• Study your objects. Hold them in your hand. What do they feel like? Bumpy? Scaley? Ribbed? Hairy? Toothy? Jagged? Smooth? Fuzzy? Etc.
• Now look at and describe what the texture looks like. Is it little circles all over that make it bumpy, or is it thin lines that are repeated?
• Give much thought to what shapes and lines work together to make the texture that you feel.
• Study “Plate 87 - Textures” on p.178.
• Watch the tutorial.
• Draw eight thumbnails in your Art Book.
• Draw a texture that you observe in each of your thumbnail boxes. Label that texture.
“Plate 87” p.178
∞ Video Link: Texture L7 L52

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

• OCCUPATIONS Do brush drawings of the same 8 textures.

Drawing: Still Life with Fabric
⍞ Materials: Charcoal, Art Book, erasers, still life objects, fabric, light source

➜ RECAP
Recall texture.

➜ PRACTICE
• Set up a still life with fabric. Make sure that the fabric has a texture. You can even use several different fabrics.
• Set up your light source.
• Draw thumbnails in order to set up an interesting composition.
• Do your drawing using charcoal in your Art Book.

Drawing: Still Life with Fabric
⍞ Materials: Pastels, Art Book, erasers, still life objects, fabric, light source

➜ RECAP
Recall texture.

➜ PRACTICE
• Set up a different still life with fabric. Make sure that the fabric has a texture. You can even use several different fabrics.
• Set up your light source.
• Draw thumbnails in order to set up an interesting composition.
• Do your drawing using pastels in your Art Book.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

• OCCUPATIONS Using this same knowledge, do a brush drawing of the same still life in your Art Book.

Drawing: Holding a Pencil
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, drawing pencils, drawing board, Art Book

➜ WARM UP
Spend three or four minutes at the chalkboard drawing with large arm motions.

➜ RECAP
Recall line.

➜ INTRO
This term, students will begin to draw with a pencil.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Different Ways to Hold the Pencil (YouTube)

➜ PRACTICE
• Draw different lines in your Art Book to practice the proper way to hold a pencil.
• Find an object in your classroom to look at and draw, holding your pencil in the different positions. Try to add shading. Do not use erasers for mistakes! Do use them as drawing tools and for highlights.

➜ DISCUSS
What was noticed or discovered?

• OCCUPATIONS Do a self-portrait using pencil. Remember to hold your pencil properly.

Drawing: Lines
⍞ Materials: Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work, chalk, chalkboard, drawing pencils, drawing board, Art Book

➜ WARM UP
Spend three or four minutes at the chalkboard drawing with large arm motions.

➜ RECAP
Recall line and design.

➜ STUDY, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
“Plate 67” and “Plate 68” p.128-129

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Line in Pictorial Composition L7 L56
∞ Image Link: Skeletal Line Example
∞ Image Link: Skeleton Line Designs

➜ PRACTICE
• Read “Line,” “Crossing Line,” “Quality of Line,” “Line in Pictorial Composition” p.120-126.
• Draw 6 thumbnails on a page in your Art Book. Create 6 different designs with lines.
• You may choose to turn these lines into floral or animal motifs, or you may choose to leave them as line designs. Or do a combination of both.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

• OCCUPATIONS Practice line designs with your watercolor and brushes.

Drawing: Value Scales in Pencil
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, drawing pencils, drawing board, Art Book

PREP: Read “Tone” Drawing, Design, and Craft-Work p.104-106.

➜ WARM UP
Spend three to four minutes drawing at the board using large arm motions.

➜ RECAP
Recall value and shading.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Value Scale with Pencil (aka Graphite) L7 L57

➜ PRACTICE
• Create a value scale with ten 1” squares.
• Using your pencils and erasers as drawing tools, create 10 different values. Start with a middle tone and work your way towards the lightest and then back towards the darkest.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

• OBSERVE & DISCUSS Practice identifying Art skills.
Study, Narrate, and Discuss
∞ Image Link: Tonal Charcoal Drawing.

• OCCUPATIONS Draw a piece of draped fabric using your pencils and erasers.

Drawing: Tonal Drawing
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, drawing board, charcoal, Art Book, pink pearl and kneaded erasers

➜ WARM UP
Spend three to four minutes drawing at the board using large arm motions.

➜ PRACTICE
• Illustrate a scene from your literature readings using charcoal and erasers.
• Keep subject matter simple so that you can do a tonal drawing similar to these examples.
∞ Image Link: Dianne Sutherland: Day 6 Tulip in Graphite
∞ Image Link: Swan

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

• OCCUPATIONS Create a page in your Art Book dedicated to handles. Find objects all around you and do observational drawings of the handles.

Drawing: Tonal Drawing
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, drawing board, charcoal, Art Book, pink pearl and kneaded erasers

➜ NOTE
This will last two drawing lessons

➜ WARM UP
Spend three to four minutes drawing at the board using large arm motions.

➜ PRACTICE
• Begin a tonal self-portrait from observation- be looking in the mirror as you work.
• Remember that in tonal drawing the value shifts are smooth. There are no observable lines from hatching and cross-hatching.
• View the examples before beginning.
∞ Image Link: Portrait Example 1
∞ Image Link: Portrait Example 2

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

• OCCUPATIONS Do another self-portrait but do it as a tonal brush drawing.

Drawing: Tonal Drawing
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, drawing board, charcoal, Art Book, pink pearl and kneaded erasers

➜ NOTE
This will last two drawing lessons

➜ WARM UP
Spend three to four minutes drawing at the board using large arm motions.

➜ PRACTICE
• Finish your tonal self-portrait from observation- be looking in the mirror as you work.
• Remember that in tonal drawing the value shifts are smooth. There are no observable lines from hatching and cross-hatching.
• View the examples before beginning.
∞ Image Link: Portrait Example 1
∞ Image Link: Portrait Example 2
∞ Image Link: Edgar Degas pastel painting (This is if you did a tonal drawing in pastel.)

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

• OCCUPATIONS Do a tonal portrait of a family member using any of your art materials.

Drawing: Still Life with Fabric
⍞ Materials: Drawing pencils, Art Book, drawing board, erasers, still life objects, fabric, light source

➜ RECAP
• Recall texture.
• Recall how to hold a pencil properly and mass drawing.
• Recall tone.

➜ PRACTICE
• Set up a still life with fabric. Make sure that the fabric has a texture. You can even use several different fabrics.
• Set up your light source.
• Draw thumbnails in order to set up an interesting composition.
• Do your drawing using pencils in your Art Book.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

Drawing: Still Life
⍞ Materials: Drawing pencils, Art Book, drawing board, spheres, cylinders and cubes, erasers

PREP: It may be time for a pep talk if students are becoming discouraged by the repetition/similarity of assignments. Just as in any discipline- music, dance, soccer, baseball, volleyball, etc.- repetitive practice is the key to becoming stronger. It is necessary to draw from observation fully focused and concentrated, as opposed to approaching a still life absent-mindedly. Observational skills and hand-eye coordination are valuable across the board. Students are growing in ways they cannot yet appreciate.

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall texture and tone.
• Recall how to use pencils properly.

➜ PRACTICE
• Set up a still life on a table in front of the window.
• Discuss observations. Where is the highlight? Point out the darkest value on each object.
• Draw the still life in your Art Book with pencils. Don’t forget the cast shadows. Are there value changes in the cast shadows?

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

• OCCUPATIONS Draw and shade spheres in your Art Book with pencils.

Drawing: Still Life
⍞ Materials: Drawing pencils, Art Book, drawing board, spheres, cylinders and cubes, textured fabric or rope, erasers, light source

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall how to draw texture and add tones.
• Recall how to use pencils properly.

➜ PRACTICE
• Students should set up an interesting still life to observe today.
• Position the light source so that it floods the front of the objects but from the side.
• Discuss observations. Where is the highlight? Point out the darkest value on each object.
• Draw the still life in your Art Book with pencils. Don’t forget the cast shadows. Are there value changes in the cast shadows?
• Don’t forget to include texture.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

Drawing: Human Model
⍞ Materials: Chalk, chalkboard, drawing pencils, drawing board, Art Book, human model

PREP: Students will need a human model today.

➜ WARM UP
Draw on the board with large arm movements.

➜ RECAP
Recall proportions of the human body and foreshortening.

➜ PRACTICE
Do an observational drawing of a friend or family member.
• Ask your model to sit in a way that is foreshortened.
• Use pencils in your Art Book, remembering the proper proportions of the human body.
• Apply what you have learned about foreshortening to the human figure.

➜ DISCUSS
Talk through the process that you went through. Share your thoughts with your teacher.

• OCCUPATIONS Continue doing more drawings of people in your Art Book, whether close-ups of heads or figures in action.

Drawing: Human Model
⍞ Materials: Drawing pencils, erasers, Art Book, drawing board, live model, lamp or spotlight

PREP: Students will need a live model for this lesson.

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, and shadows.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.
• Recall proportions of the head and bust.

➜ PRACTICE
• Have your model sit, and place the light source pointing towards their head, but from behind.
• Study the effect of light on the model's head.
• Discuss observations. Where are the highlights? Point out the darkest values on their head.
• Do an observational drawing of your model, focusing on the head, neck, and shoulders, and applying all of your knowledge about shading and highlights.

➜ DISCUSS
Tell what was noticed or discovered.

Drawing: Human Model
⍞ Materials: Drawing pencils, Art Book, drawing board, erasers

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, shadows, texture, and tones.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.
• Recall proportions of the head and bust.

➜ PRACTICE
• Do a drawing of a friend or family member from memory, focusing on the head, neck, and shoulders, and applying all of your knowledge about shading, texture, tones, and highlights.
• Show your drawing to the person that you drew.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare your drawing to real life. Discuss how you did.

Drawing: Realistic Pencil Drawing
⍞ Materials: Drawing pencils, Art Book, drawing board, erasers, paper towel

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, shadows, texture, and tones.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Realistic Pencil Drawing (YouTube)

➜ PRACTICE
• Watch the tutorial in full.
• Try drawing water in a clear container, like what The Virtual Instructor did in the video.
• You have the remaining time in this lesson and the next lesson to work on this.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare your drawing to real life. Discuss how you did.

Drawing: Realistic Pencil Drawing
⍞ Materials: Drawing pencils, Art Book, drawing board, erasers, paper towel

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, shadows, texture, and tones.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.

➜ PRACTICE
• Try drawing water in a clear container, like what The Virtual Instructor did in the video.

➜ SELF-EVALUATE
Compare your drawing to real life. Discuss how you did.

Drawing: Realistic Pencil Drawing
⍞ Materials: Drawing pencils, Art Book, drawing board, erasers, paper towel

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, shadows, texture, and tones.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.

➜ NOTE
You have learned many new ideas in this and the last two terms. Take the next two lesson times to create your own works of art, applying the knowledge you have gained.

➜ PRACTICE
Create works of art based on your own interest, applying the knowledge you have gained.

Drawing: Realistic Pencil Drawing
⍞ Materials: Drawing pencils, Art Book, drawing board, erasers, paper towel

➜ RECAP
• Recall light source, shading, shadows, texture, and tones.
• Recall highlights and cast shadows.

➜ NOTE
You have learned many new ideas in this and the last two terms. Take the next two lesson times to create your own works of art, applying the knowledge you have gained.

Create works of art based on your own interest, applying the knowledge you have gained.
